parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 1–13

passage.text: Stepwells

A millennium ago, stepwells were fundamental to life in the driest parts of India. Although many have been neglected, recent restoration has returned them to their former glory. Richard Cox travelled to north-western India to document these spectacular monuments from a bygone era.

During the sixth and seventh centuries, the inhabitants of the modern-day states of Gujarat and Rajasthan in North-western India developed a method of gaining access to clean, fresh groundwater during the dry season for drinking, bathing, watering animals and irrigation. However, the significance of this invention – the stepwell – goes beyond its utilitarian application.

Unique to the region, stepwells are often architecturally complex and vary widely in size and shape. During their heyday, they were places of gathering, of leisure, of relaxation and of worship for villagers of all but the lowest castes. Most stepwells are found dotted around the desert areas of Gujarat (where they are called vav) and Rajasthan (where they are known as baori), while a few also survive in Delhi. Some were located in or near villages as public spaces for the community; others were positioned beside roads as resting places for travellers.

As their name suggests, stepwells comprise a series of stone steps descending from ground level to the water source (normally an underground aquifer) as it recedes following the rains. When the water level was high, the user needed only to descend a few steps to reach it; when it was low, several levels would have to be negotiated.

Some wells are vast, open craters with hundreds of steps paving each sloping side, often in tiers. Others are more elaborate, with long stepped passages leading to the water via several storeys. Built from stone and supported by pillars, they also included pavilions that sheltered visitors from the relentless heat. But perhaps the most impressive features are the intricate decorative sculptures that embellish many stepwells, showing activities from fighting and dancing to everyday acts such as women combing their hair and churning butter.

Down the centuries, thousands of wells were constructed throughout northwestern India, but the majority have now fallen into disuse; many are derelict and dry, as groundwater has been diverted for industrial use and the wells no longer reach the water table. Their condition hasn’t been helped by recent dry spells: southern Rajasthan suffered an eight-year drought between 1996 and 2004.
However, some important sites in Gujarat have recently undergone major restoration, and the state government announced in June last year that it plans to restore the stepwells throughout the state.

In Patan, the state’s ancient capital, the stepwell of Rani Ki Vav (Queen’s Stepwell) is perhaps the finest current example. It was built by Queen Udayamati during the late 11th century, but became silted up following a flood during the 13th century. But the Archaeological Survey of India began restoring it in the 1960s, and today it’s in pristine condition. At 65 metres long, 20 metres wide and 27 metres deep, Rani Ki Vav features 500 distinct sculptures carved into niches throughout the monument, depicting gods such as Vishnu and Parvati in various incarnations. Incredibly, in January 2001, this ancient structure survived a devastating earthquake that measured 7.6 on the Richter scale.

Another example is the Surya Kund in Modhera, northern Gujarat, next to the Sun Temple, built by King Bhima I in 1026 to honour the sun god Surya. It actually resembles a tank (kund means reservoir or pond) rather than a well, but displays the hallmarks of stepwell architecture, including four sides of steps that descend to the bottom in a stunning geometrical formation. The terraces house 108 small, intricately carved shrines between the sets of steps.

Rajasthan also has a wealth of wells. The ancient city of Bundi, 200 kilometres south of Jaipur, is renowned for its architecture, including its stepwells. One of the larger examples is Raniji Ki Baori, which was built by the queen of the region, Nathavatji, in 1699. At 46 metres deep, 20 metres wide and 40 metres long, the intricately carved monument is one of 21 baoris commissioned in the Bundi area by Nathavatji.

In the old ruined town of Abhaneri, about 95 kilometres east of Jaipur, is Chand Baori, one of India’s oldest and deepest wells; aesthetically, it’s perhaps one of the most dramatic. Built in around 850 AD next to the temple of Harshat Mata, the baori comprises hundreds of zigzagging steps that run along three of its sides, steeply descending 11 storeys, resulting in a striking geometric pattern when seen from afar. On the fourth side, verandas which are supported by ornate pillars overlook the steps.

Still in public use is Neemrana Ki Baori, located just off the Jaipur–Delhi highway. Constructed in around 1700, it’s nine storeys deep, with the last two levels underwater. At ground level, there are 86 colonnaded openings from where the visitor descends 170 steps to the deepest water source.

Today, following years of neglect, many of these monuments to medieval engineering have been saved by the Archaeological Survey of India, which has recognised the importance of preserving them as part of the country’s rich history. Tourists flock to wells in far-flung corners of northwestern India to gaze in wonder at these architectural marvels from 1,000 years ago, which serve as a reminder of both the ingenuity and artistry of ancient civilisations and of the value of water to human existence.

parts.passageHtml:

Today, following years of neglect, many of these monuments to medieval engineering have been saved by the Archaeological Survey of India, which has recognised the importance of preserving them as part of the country’s rich history. Tourists flock to wells in far-flung corners of northwestern India to gaze in wonder at these architectural marvels from 1,000 years ago, which serve as a reminder of both the ingenuity and artistry of ancient civilisations and of the value of water to human existence.

questionGroups.instruction: Questions 1–5Do the following statements agree with the information given in the Reading Passage?In boxes 1–5 on your answer sheet, writeTRUE if the statement agrees with the informationFALSE if the statement contradicts the informationNOT GIVEN if there is no information on this
questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 1–5

Do the following statements agree with the information given in the Reading Passage?

In boxes 1–5 on your answer sheet, write
TRUE  if the statement agrees with the information
FALSE  if the statement contradicts the information
NOT GIVEN  if there is no information on this
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: Examples of ancient stepwells can be found all over the world.
questions.explanation:
Câu 1: FALSE.

Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Xác định keyword trong câu hỏi

“examples of ancient stepwells”
“all over the world”

Bước 2: Scan bài đọc tìm đoạn nói về vị trí của stepwells

Đoạn quan trọng nằm ở paragraph 2:

“Unique to the region, stepwells are often architecturally complex…”

và:

“Most stepwells are found dotted around the desert areas of Gujarat … and Rajasthan … while a few also survive in Delhi.”

Bước 3: Phân tích meaning

Câu hỏi nói:

“Examples of ancient stepwells can be found all over the world.”

= Stepwells tồn tại khắp thế giới.

Nhưng bài đọc nói:

“Unique to the region”

= Chỉ đặc trưng cho khu vực đó (Ấn Độ vùng Gujarat/Rajasthan).

Tức là không phải “all over the world”.

Bước 4: Kết luận dạng TRUE/FALSE/NOT GIVEN

Statement nói phạm vi toàn thế giới.
Passage nói chỉ riêng khu vực đó.

→ Thông tin bị contradicted.

=> Đáp án: FALSE.

Lý do nhiều người dễ sai:

Thấy “thousands of wells” rồi tưởng phổ biến toàn cầu.
Nhưng keyword quyết định là “all over the world”.
Passage không hề nói toàn thế giới, mà còn nhấn mạnh “unique to the region”.

questions.prompt: Stepwells had a range of functions, in addition to those related to water collection.
questions.explanation:
TRUE.

Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Xác định keyword trong câu hỏi

“a range of functions”
“in addition to”
“water collection”

“In addition to” = ngoài việc lấy nước ra còn có chức năng khác.

→ Đây là dạng paraphrase cực phổ biến.

Bước 2: Scan bài đọc tìm đoạn nói về chức năng của stepwells

Đoạn quan trọng nằm ở paragraph 2:

“they were places of gathering, of leisure, of relaxation and of worship”

Bước 3: Phân tích meaning

Câu hỏi nói:

“Stepwells had a range of functions, in addition to those related to water collection.”

= Stepwells có nhiều chức năng ngoài việc liên quan đến nước.

Passage nói:

gathering = tụ tập
leisure = giải trí
relaxation = thư giãn
worship = thờ cúng

→ Rõ ràng có nhiều chức năng xã hội và tôn giáo ngoài lấy nước.

Bước 4: Kết luận

Statement và passage cùng meaning.

=> TRUE.

Lý do nhiều người dễ sai:

Nhiều bạn chỉ đọc paragraph đầu rồi nghĩ stepwells chỉ để lấy nước.
Nhưng IELTS thường đặt đáp án ở đoạn sau ngay cùng paragraph.
Từ “functions” được paraphrase thành list activities.

questions.prompt: The few existing stepwells in Delhi are more attractive than those found elsewhere.
questions.explanation:
NOT GIVEN.

Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Xác định keyword

“existing stepwells in Delhi”
“more attractive”
“than those found elsewhere”

Đây là dạng comparison.

Bước 2: Scan bài đọc tìm “Delhi”

Đoạn chứa keyword:

“while a few also survive in Delhi.”

Bước 3: Kiểm tra xem có comparison không

Question nói:

“The few existing stepwells in Delhi are more attractive than those found elsewhere.”

= Stepwells ở Delhi đẹp hơn nơi khác.

Nhưng passage chỉ nói:

có một vài cái còn tồn tại ở Delhi.

KHÔNG nói:

đẹp hơn
hấp dẫn hơn
nổi tiếng hơn
xấu hơn

Không có bất kỳ sự so sánh nào.

Bước 4: Kết luận

IELTS rule:

Có keyword ≠ có đáp án.
Phải đúng toàn bộ meaning.

Vì bài không cho thông tin so sánh.

=> NOT GIVEN.

Lý do nhiều người dễ sai:

Thấy có “Delhi” là khoanh TRUE.
Nhưng TRUE/FALSE/NG phải kiểm tra:
có đúng keyword không
có đúng meaning không
có đủ thông tin không

Ở đây chỉ match keyword, không match idea.

questions.prompt: It took workers many years to build the stone steps characteristic of stepwells.
questions.explanation:
Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Xác định keyword

“took workers many years”
“build”
“stone steps”

Bước 2: Scan bài đọc tìm đoạn nói về steps

Đoạn liên quan:

“stepwells comprise a series of stone steps descending from ground level to the water source”

Bước 3: So sánh meaning

Question nói:

công nhân mất nhiều năm để xây các bậc đá.

Passage chỉ mô tả:

có các bậc đá.

Passage KHÔNG nói:

ai xây
mất bao lâu
quá trình xây dựng

Bước 4: Kết luận

Không đủ thông tin để xác nhận hay phủ định.

=> NOT GIVEN.

Lý do nhiều người dễ sai:

Thấy công trình lớn nên tự suy luận “chắc xây lâu”.
Nhưng IELTS cấm dùng kiến thức ngoài bài.
Chỉ được dùng information explicitly stated.

questions.prompt: The number of steps above the water level in a stepwell altered during the course of a year.
questions.explanation:
Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Xác định keyword

“number of steps”
“above the water level”
“altered during the course of a year”

“altered” = changed.

Bước 2: Scan đoạn nói về water level

Đoạn chứa đáp án:

“When the water level was high, the user needed only to descend a few steps to reach it; when it was low, several levels would have to be negotiated.”

Bước 3: Phân tích meaning

Khi nước cao:

chỉ cần đi xuống vài bậc.

Khi nước thấp:

phải đi xuống nhiều tầng/bậc hơn.

Điều này có nghĩa:

số bậc nằm phía trên mặt nước thay đổi theo mực nước.

Bước 4: Kết luận

Meaning hoàn toàn khớp với statement.

=> TRUE.

Lý do nhiều người dễ sai:

Không nhận ra paraphrase:
altered = changed
several levels = more steps
IELTS thường không dùng lại nguyên từ.

questionGroups.instruction: Questions 6–8Complete the sentences below.Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.blockHtml:
• Which part of some stepwells provided shade for people? 6_______

• What type of serious climatic event, which took place in southern Rajasthan, is mentioned in the article? 7_______

• Who are frequent visitors to stepwells nowadays? 8_______
questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 6–8

Complete the sentences below.

Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: Which part of some stepwells provided shade for people?
questions.explanation:
Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Đọc câu hỏi và xác định keyword

Question:

Which part of some stepwells provided shade for people?

Keyword:

provided shade
people

“provided shade” = tạo bóng mát / che nắng.

Bước 2: Scan bài đọc tìm synonym

Đoạn quan trọng:

“they also included pavilions that sheltered visitors from the relentless heat.”

Bước 3: Match paraphrase

Question:

provided shade for people

Passage:

sheltered visitors from the heat

Paraphrase:

sheltered from heat = che nắng / tạo bóng mát
visitors = people

Bước 4: Xác định đáp án đúng grammar

Question hỏi:

Which part...

→ cần noun.

Trong câu:

pavilions

=> đáp án: pavilions

Lý do dễ sai:

Nhiều bạn chọn “pillars” vì thấy liên quan construction.
Nhưng “pillars” không có chức năng che nắng.
questions.questionHtml:
• Which part of some stepwells provided shade for people? 6_______

• What type of serious climatic event, which took place in southern Rajasthan, is mentioned in the article? 7_______

• Who are frequent visitors to stepwells nowadays? 8_______

questions.prompt: What type of serious climatic event, which took place in southern Rajasthan, is mentioned in the article?
questions.explanation:
Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Xác định keyword

Question:

What type of serious climatic event...

Keyword:

climatic event
southern Rajasthan

Bước 2: Scan đúng location

Đoạn chứa đáp án:

“southern Rajasthan suffered an eight-year drought between 1996 and 2004.”

Bước 3: Match meaning

Question:

serious climatic event

Passage:

drought

Drought = hạn hán → hiện tượng khí hậu nghiêm trọng.

Bước 4: Kiểm tra word limit

Instruction:

ONE WORD ONLY

=> drought

Lý do dễ sai:

Một số bạn viết “eight-year drought”.
Nhưng đề chỉ cho ONE WORD ONLY.

questions.prompt: Who are frequent visitors to stepwells nowadays?
questions.explanation:
Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Xác định keyword

Question:

Who are frequent visitors to stepwells nowadays?

Keyword:

nowadays
frequent visitors

Bước 2: Scan đoạn cuối vì “nowadays/today” thường nằm cuối passage

Đoạn:

“Tourists flock to wells in far-flung corners of northwestern India…”

Bước 3: Match paraphrase

Question:

frequent visitors

Passage:

flock to

“flock to” = kéo đến đông đảo thường xuyên.

Bước 4: Chọn đáp án đúng grammar

Question:

Who are...

→ cần noun chỉ người.

=> tourists

Lý do dễ sai:

Có bạn ghi “visitors”.
Nhưng phải lấy từ passage, không tự paraphrase đáp án.

questionGroups.instruction: Questions 9–13Complete the table below.Choose ONE WORD AND/OR A NUMBER from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.blockHtml:
StepwellsDateFeaturesOther notes
Rani Ki VvrLate 11th centuryAs many as 500 sculptures decorate the monumentRestored in the 1990s



Excellent condition, despite the 9_______ of 2001.

Carved shrines.		Looks more like a 11_______ than a well.
Raniji Ki Baori1699Intricately carved monumentOne of 21 baoris in the area commissioned by Queen Nathavatji

Has 12_______ which provide a view to the steps.

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 9–13

Complete the table below.

Choose ONE WORD AND/OR A NUMBER from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: Rani Ki Vav — Survived the ___ of 2001.
questions.explanation:
Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Đọc table carefully

Sentence:

Excellent condition, despite the ____ of 2001.

Keyword:

2001

Bước 2: Scan bài tìm năm 2001

Đoạn:

“Incredibly, in January 2001, this ancient structure survived a devastating earthquake…”

Bước 3: Match grammar

Sentence:

despite the ____ of 2001

→ cần noun.

“earthquake” fit perfectly.

=> earthquake

Lý do dễ sai:

Nhiều bạn viết “devastating earthquake”.
Nhưng ONE WORD ONLY.
questions.questionHtml:
StepwellsDateFeaturesOther notes
Rani Ki VvrLate 11th centuryAs many as 500 sculptures decorate the monumentRestored in the 1990s



Excellent condition, despite the 9_______ of 2001.
Surya Kund1026Steps on the 10_______ produce a geometric pattern
Carved shrines.		Looks more like a 11_______ than a well.
Raniji Ki Baori1699Intricately carved monumentOne of 21 baoris in the area commissioned by Queen Nathavatji
Chand Baori850 ADSteps take you down 11 storeys to the bottomOld, deep and very dramatic



Has 12_______ which provide a view to the steps.
Neemrana Ki Baori1700Has two
13_______ levels.		Used by public today

questions.prompt: Surya Kund — Steps on the ___ sides produce a geometric pattern.
questions.explanation:
Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Đọc câu trong bảng

Steps on the ____ produce a geometric pattern.

Keyword:

geometric pattern

Bước 2: Scan passage tìm “geometric”

Đoạn:

“four sides of steps that descend to the bottom in a stunning geometrical formation.”

Bước 3: Match meaning

Question:

produce a geometric pattern

Passage:

geometrical formation

→ cùng meaning.

Bước 4: Xác định noun cần điền

“Steps on the ____”

→ cần noun plural.

Trong passage:

four sides of steps

=> 4 sides

questions.prompt: Surya Kund — Looks more like a ___ than a well.
questions.explanation:
Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Đọc sentence

Looks more like a ____ than a well.

Bước 2: Scan đoạn Surya Kund

Đoạn:

“It actually resembles a tank rather than a well”

Bước 3: Match paraphrase

Question:

looks more like

Passage:

resembles

=> paraphrase trực tiếp.

Bước 4: Lấy đáp án

=> tank

Lý do dễ sai:

Một số bạn viết “reservoir”.
Nhưng “reservoir” nằm trong dấu ngoặc giải thích “kund means reservoir”.
Câu so sánh trực tiếp dùng “tank”.

questions.prompt: Chand Baori — Has ___ which provide a view of the steps.
questions.explanation:
Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Đọc câu hỏi trong table

Has ____ which provide a view of the steps.

Keyword:

provide a view

Bước 2: Scan đoạn Chand Baori

Đoạn:

“verandas which are supported by ornate pillars overlook the steps.”

Bước 3: Match paraphrase

Question:

provide a view of

Passage:

overlook

“overlook” = nhìn ra / có view.

Bước 4: Chọn đáp án đúng grammar

Question:

Has ____ which provide...

→ cần plural noun.

=> verandas

Lý do dễ sai:

Có bạn chọn “pillars”.
Nhưng pillars support the verandas, không phải nơi nhìn xuống steps.

questions.prompt: Neemrana Ki Baori — Has two ___ levels.
questions.explanation:
Cách làm từng bước:

Bước 1: Đọc câu trong bảng

Has two ____ levels.

Keyword:

two levels

Bước 2: Scan đoạn Neemrana Ki Baori

Đoạn:

“with the last two levels underwater.”

Bước 3: Kiểm tra grammar

Question:

two ____ levels

→ cần adjective.

“underwater” là adjective mô tả levels.

=> underwater

Lý do dễ sai:

Có bạn viết “water”.
Nhưng passage dùng “underwater”, và grammar của câu cũng cần adjective.

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 14–26

passage.text: European Transport Systems 1990-2010
What have been the trends and what are the prospects for European transport systems?

It is difficult to conceive of vigorous economic growth without an efficient transport system. Although modern information technologies can reduce the demand for physical transport by facilitating teleworking and teleservices, the requirement for transport continues to increase. There are two key factors behind this trend. For passenger transport, the determining factor is the spectacular growth in car use. The number of cars on European Union (EU) roads saw an increase of three million cars each year from 1990 to 2010, and in the next decade the EU will see a further substantial increase in its fleet.

As far as goods transport is concerned, growth is due to a large extent to changes in the European economy and its system of production. In the last 20 years, as internal frontiers have been abolished, the EU has moved from a ‘stock’ economy to a ‘flow’ economy. This phenomenon has been emphasised by the relocation of some industries, particularly those which are labour intensive, to reduce production costs, even though the production site is hundreds or even thousands of kilometres away from the final assembly plant or away from users.

The strong economic growth expected in countries which are candidates for entry to the EU will also increase transport flows, in particular road haulage traffic. In 1998, some of these countries already exported more than twice their 1990 volumes and imported more than five times their 1990 volumes. And although many candidate countries inherited a transport system which encourages rail, the distribution between modes has tipped sharply in favour of road transport since the 1990s. Between 1990 and 1998, road haulage increased by 19.4%, while during the same period rail haulage decreased by 43.5%, although – and this could benefit the enlarged EU – it is still on average at a much higher level than in existing member states.

However, a new imperative-sustainable development – offers an opportunity for adapting the EU’s common transport policy. This objective, agreed by the Gothenburg European Council, has to be achieved by integrating environmental considerations into Community policies, and shifting the balance between modes of transport lies at the heart of its strategy. The ambitious objective can only be fully achieved by 2020, but proposed measures are nonetheless a first essential step towards a sustainable transport system which will ideally be in place in 30 years’ time, that is by 2040.

In 1998, energy consumption in the transport sector was to blame for 28% of emissions of CO2, the leading greenhouse gas. According to the latest estimates, if nothing is done to reverse the traffic growth trend, CO2 emissions from transport can be expected to increase by around 50% to 1,113 billion tonnes by 2020, compared with the 739 billion tonnes recorded in 1990. Once again, road transport is the main culprit since it alone accounts for 84% of the CO2 emissions attributable to transport. Using alternative fuels and improving energy efficiency is thus both an ecological necessity and a technological challenge.

At the same time greater efforts must be made to achieve a modal shift. Such a change cannot be achieved overnight, all the less so after over half a century of constant deterioration in favour of road. This has reached such a pitch that today rail freight services are facing marginalisation, with just 8% of market share, and with international goods trains struggling along at an average speed of 18km/h. Three possible options have emerged.

The first approach would consist of focusing on road transport solely through pricing. This option would not be accompanied by complementary measures in the other modes of transport. In the short term it might curb the growth in road transport through the better loading ratio of goods vehicles and occupancy rates of passenger vehicles expected as a result of the increase in the price of transport. However, the lack of measures available to revitalise other modes of transport would make it impossible for more sustainable modes of transport to take up the baton.

The second approach also concentrates on road transport pricing but is accompanied by measures to increase the efficiency of the other modes (better quality of services, logistics, technology). However, this approach does not include investment in new infrastructure, nor does it guarantee better regional cohesion. It could help to achieve greater uncoupling than the first approach, but road transport would keep the lion’s share of the market and continue to concentrate on saturated arteries, despite being the most polluting of the modes. It is therefore not enough to guarantee the necessary shift of the balance.

The third approach, which is not new, comprises a series of measures ranging from pricing to revitalising alternative modes of transport and targeting investment in the trans-European network. This integrated approach would allow the market shares of the other modes to return to their 1998 levels and thus make a shift of balance. It is far more ambitious than it looks, bearing in mind the historical imbalance in favour of roads for the last fifty years, but would achieve a marked break in the link between road transport growth and economic growth, without placing restrictions on the mobility of people and goods.

However, this approach does not include investment in new infrastructure, nor does it guarantee better regional cohesion. It is therefore not enough to guarantee the necessary shift of the balance.

The third approach, which is not new, comprises a series of measures ranging from pricing to revitalising alternative modes of transport and targeting investment in the trans-European network. This integrated approach would allow the market shares of the other modes to return to their 1998 levels and thus make a shift of balance. It is far more ambitious than it looks, bearing in mind the historical imbalance in favour of roads for the last fifty years, but would achieve a marked break in the link between road transport growth and economic growth, without placing restrictions on the mobility of people and goods.

questionGroups.instruction: Questions 14–21Reading Passage has paragraphs A–H. Choose the correct heading for each paragraph from the list of headings below.Write the correct number, i–xi, in boxes 14–21 on your answer sheet.
questionGroups.blockHtml:
List of Headings

  1. A fresh and important long-term goal
  2. Charging for roads and improving other transport methods
  3. Changes affecting the distances goods may be transported
  4. Taking all the steps necessary to change transport patterns
  5. The environmental costs of road transport
  6. The escalating cost of rail transport
  7. The need to achieve transport rebalance
  8. The rapid growth of private transport
  9. Plans to develop major road networks
  10. Restricting road use through charging policies alone
  11. Transport trends in countries awaiting EU admission

questions.explanation: Paragraph A focuses on the rapid growth in car use on EU roads — three million new cars per year.

questions.prompt: Paragraph B
questions.explanation: Paragraph B discusses how industry relocation means production sites are 'hundreds or even thousands of kilometres away from the final assembly plant', affecting transport distances.

questions.prompt: Paragraph C
questions.explanation: Paragraph C discusses transport trends in countries that are candidates for EU membership.

questions.prompt: Paragraph D
questions.explanation: Paragraph D presents sustainable development as a new long-term goal, to be achieved by 2040 — a fresh and important objective.

questions.prompt: Paragraph E
questions.explanation: Paragraph E deals with CO₂ emissions and the environmental costs attributable to road transport.

questions.prompt: Paragraph G
questions.explanation: Paragraph G describes the first approach: restricting road use through pricing alone, with no complementary measures for other modes.

questions.prompt: Paragraph H
questions.explanation: Paragraph H describes road pricing accompanied by measures to increase efficiency of other transport modes.

questions.prompt: Paragraph I
questions.explanation: Paragraph I describes the integrated third approach: a series of measures from pricing to revitalising alternatives — taking all necessary steps.

questions.prompt: The need for transport is growing, despite technological developments.
questions.explanation: 'Although modern information technologies can reduce the demand for physical transport...the requirement for transport continues to increase.'

questions.prompt: To reduce production costs, some industries have been moved closer to their relevant consumers.
questions.explanation: The passage states production sites are 'hundreds or even thousands of kilometres away from the final assembly plant or away from users' — they moved FARTHER, not closer.

questions.prompt: Cars are prohibitively expensive in some EU candidate countries.
questions.explanation: The passage makes no mention of car prices in EU candidate countries.

questions.prompt: The Gothenburg European Council was set up 30 years ago.
questions.explanation: The passage mentions the Gothenburg European Council agreed on a sustainable development objective, but gives no date for when the Council was established.

questions.prompt: By the end of this decade, CO₂ emissions from transport are predicted to reach 739 billion tonnes.
questions.explanation: 739 billion tonnes was the 1990 figure. The passage predicts emissions will increase to 1,113 billion tonnes by 2020.

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 27–40

passage.text: The psychology of innovation
Why are so few companies truly innovative?

Innovation is key to business survival, and companies put substantial resources into inspiring employees to develop new ideas. There are, nevertheless, people working in luxurious, state-of-the-art centres designed to stimulate innovation who find that their environment doesn’t make them feel at all creative. And there are those who don’t have a budget, or much space, but who innovate successfully.

For Robert B. Cialdini, Professor of Psychology at Arizona State University, one reason that companies don’t succeed as often as they should is that innovation starts with recruitment. Research shows that the fit between an employee’s values and a company’s values makes a difference to what contribution they make and whether, two years after they join, they’re still at the company. Studies at Harvard Business School show that, although some individuals may be more creative than others, almost every individual can be creative in the right circumstances.

One of the most famous photographs in the story of rock’n’roll emphasises Ciaidini’s views. The 1956 picture of singers Elvis Presley, Carl Perkins, Johnny Cash and Jerry Lee Lewis jamming at a piano in Sun Studios in Memphis tells a hidden story. Sun’s ‘million-dollar quartet’ could have been a quintet. Missing from the picture is Roy Orbison’ a greater natural singer than Lewis, Perkins or Cash. Sam Phillips, who owned Sun, wanted to revolutionise popular music with songs that fused black and white music, and country and blues. Presley, Cash, Perkins and Lewis instinctively understood Phillips’s ambition and believed in it. Orbison wasn’t inspired by the goal, and only ever achieved one hit with the Sun label.

The value fit matters, says Cialdini, because innovation is, in part, a process of change, and under that pressure we, as a species, behave differently, ‘When things change, we are hard-wired to play it safe.’ Managers should therefore adopt an approach that appears counterintuitive -they should explain what stands to be lost if the company fails to seize a particular opportunity. Studies show that we invariably take more gambles when threatened with a loss than when offered a reward.

Managing innovation is a delicate art. It’s easy for a company to be pulled in conflicting directions as the marketing, product development, and finance departments each get different feedback from different sets of people. And without a system which ensures collaborative exchanges within the company, it’s also easy for small ‘pockets of innovation’ to disappear. Innovation is a contact sport. You can’t brief people just by saying, ‘We’re going in this direction and I’m going to take you with me.’

Cialdini believes that this ‘follow-the-leader syndrome, is dangerous, not least because it encourages bosses to go it alone. ‘It’s been scientifically proven that three people will be better than one at solving problems, even if that one person is the smartest person in the field.’ To prove his point, Cialdini cites an interview with molecular biologist James Watson. Watson, together with Francis Crick, discovered the structure of DNA, the genetic information carrier of all living organisms. ‘When asked how they had cracked the code ahead of an array of highly accomplished rival investigators, he said something that stunned me. He said he and Crick had succeeded because they were aware that they weren’t the most intelligent of the scientists pursuing the answer. The smartest scientist was called Rosalind Franklin who, Watson said, “was so intelligent she rarely sought advice”.’

Teamwork taps into one of the basic drivers of human behaviour. ‘The principle of social proof is so pervasive that we don’t even recognise it,’ says Cialdini. ‘If your project is being resisted, for example, by a group of veteran employees, ask another old-timer to speak up for it.’ Cialdini is not alone in advocating this strategy. Research shows that peer power, used horizontally not vertically, is much more powerful than any boss’s speech.

Writing, visualising and prototyping can stimulate the flow of new ideas. Cialdini cites scores of research papers and historical events that prove that even something as simple as writing deepens every individual’s engagement in the project. It is, he says, the reason why all those competitions on breakfast cereal packets encouraged us to write in saying, in no more than 10 words: ‘I like Kellogg’s Com Flakes because… .’ The very act of writing makes us more likely to believe it.

Authority doesn’t have to inhibit innovation but it often does. The wrong kind of leadership will lead to what Cialdini calls ‘captainitis, the regrettable tendency of team members to opt out of team responsibilities that are properly theirs’. He calls it captainitis because, he says, ‘crew members of multipilot aircraft exhibit a sometimes deadly passivity when the flight captain makes a clearly wrong-headed decision’. This behaviour is not, he says, unique to air travel, but can happen in any workplace where the leader is overbearing.

At the other end of the scale is the 1980s Memphis design collective, a group of young designers for whom ‘the only rule was that there were no rule’. This environment encouraged a free interchange of ideas, which led to more creativity with form, function, colour and materials that revolutionised attitudes to furniture design.

Many theorists believe the ideal boss should lead from behind, taking pride in collective accomplishment and giving credit where it is due. Cialdini says: ‘Leaders should encourage everyone to contribute and simultaneously assure all concerned that every recommendation is important to making the right decision and will be given full attention.’ The frustrating thing about innovation is that there are many approaches, but no magic formula. However, a manager who wants to create a truly innovative culture can make their job a lot easier by recognising these psychological realities.

The psychology of innovation

Why are so few companies truly innovative?

A.

Innovation is key to business survival, and companies put substantial resources into inspiring employees to develop new ideas. There are, nevertheless, people working in luxurious, state-of-the-art centres designed to stimulate innovation who find that their environment doesn’t make them feel at all creative. And there are those who don’t have a budget, or much space, but who innovate successfully.

However, a manager who wants to create a truly innovative culture can make their job a lot easier by recognising these psychological realities.

**A type of parrot:**

questions.prompt: The example of the 'million-dollar quartet' underlines the writer's point about
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
million-dollar quartet
writer’s point
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 3:
“Presley, Cash, Perkins and Lewis instinctively understood Phillips’s ambition and believed in it.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
understood Phillips’s ambition = có cùng mục tiêu
believed in it = shared objective
Ví dụ này nhấn mạnh rằng các thành viên thành công vì cùng chia sẻ tầm nhìn.
=> Đáp án: C

questions.prompt: James Watson suggests that he and Francis Crick won the race to discover the DNA code because they

options.label: were conscious of their own limitations.
brought complementary skills to their partnership.
were determined to outperform their brighter rivals.
encouraged each other to realise their joint ambition.

questions.explanation:
Watson and Crick
why they succeeded
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 6:
“he and Crick had succeeded because they were aware that they weren’t the most intelligent...”
Bước 3: Phân tích
aware that they weren’t the most intelligent = nhận thức được giới hạn của bản thân
=> họ thành công vì biết mình không phải người thông minh nhất nên sẵn sàng hợp tác/tìm lời khuyên.
=> Đáp án: A

questions.prompt: The writer mentions competitions on breakfast cereal packets as an example of how to
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
breakfast cereal packets
example
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 8:
“The very act of writing makes us more likely to believe it.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
writing = viết ra ý tưởng
more likely to believe it = tăng sự cam kết với ý tưởng
=> ví dụ này chứng minh việc viết giúp củng cố niềm tin/cam kết.
=> Đáp án: D

questions.prompt: In the last paragraph, the writer suggests that it is important for employees to

options.label: be aware of their company's goals.
feel that their contributions are valued.
have respect for their co-workers' achievements.
understand why certain management decisions are made.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
last paragraph
important for employees
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn cuối:
“every recommendation is important... and will be given full attention.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
every recommendation is important = mọi đóng góp đều được coi trọng
given full attention = được lắng nghe
=> nhân viên cần cảm thấy đóng góp của họ có giá trị.
=> Đáp án: B

questionGroups.instruction: Questions 31–35Complete each sentence with the correct ending, A–G, below.Write the correct letter in boxes 31–35 on your answer sheet.
questionGroups.blockHtml:
Employees whose values match those of their employers are more likely to 31_______

At times of change, people tend to 32_______

If people are aware of what they might lose, they will often 33_______

People working under a dominant boss are liable to 34_______

Employees working in organisations with few rules are more likely to 35_______

List of options

A. take chances.

B. share their ideas.

C. become competitive.

D. get promotion.

E. avoid risk.

F. ignore their duties.

G. remain in their jobs.
questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 31–35

Complete each sentence with the correct ending, A–G, below.

Write the correct letter in boxes 31–35 on your answer sheet.

share their ideas.

questions.prompt: Employees whose values match those of their employers are more likely to
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
values match
employers
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 2:
“whether, two years after they join, they’re still at the company.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
still at the company = remain in their jobs
=> nhân viên hợp giá trị công ty thường ở lại lâu hơn.
=> Đáp án: remain in their jobs
questions.questionHtml:
Employees whose values match those of their employers are more likely to 31_______

At times of change, people tend to 32_______

If people are aware of what they might lose, they will often 33_______

People working under a dominant boss are liable to 34_______

Employees working in organisations with few rules are more likely to 35_______

List of options

A. take chances.

B. share their ideas.

C. become competitive.

D. get promotion.

E. avoid risk.

F. ignore their duties.

G. remain in their jobs.

questions.prompt: At times of change, people tend to
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
times of change
people tend to
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 4:
“When things change, we are hard-wired to play it safe.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
play it safe = avoid risk
=> khi có thay đổi, con người thường tránh rủi ro.
=> Đáp án: avoid risk

questions.prompt: If people are aware of what they might lose, they will often
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
aware of what they might lose
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 4:
“we invariably take more gambles when threatened with a loss”
Bước 3: Phân tích
take more gambles = take chances
threatened with a loss = biết mình có thể mất gì
=> con người dễ chấp nhận mạo hiểm hơn.
=> Đáp án: take chances

questions.prompt: People working under a dominant boss are liable to
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
dominant boss
liable to
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 9:
“team members to opt out of team responsibilities”
Bước 3: Phân tích
opt out of responsibilities = ignore their duties
=> dưới sếp quá áp đặt, nhân viên dễ bỏ mặc trách nhiệm.
=> Đáp án: ignore their duties

questions.prompt: Employees working in organisations with few rules are more likely to
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
few rules
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 10:
“free interchange of ideas”
Bước 3: Phân tích
free interchange of ideas = share their ideas
=> môi trường ít quy tắc khuyến khích chia sẻ ý tưởng.
=> Đáp án: share their ideas

questionGroups.instruction: Questions 36–40Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in the Reading Passage?In boxes 36–40 on your answer sheet, writeYES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writerNO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writerNOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this
questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 36–40

Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in the Reading Passage?

In boxes 36–40 on your answer sheet, write
YES  if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
NO  if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
NOT GIVEN  if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: The physical surroundings in which a person works play a key role in determining their creativity.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
physical surroundings
key role
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 1:
“there are people working in luxurious... centres... who find that their environment doesn’t make them feel at all creative.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Bài đọc nói môi trường đẹp chưa chắc tạo sáng tạo.
=> phủ nhận việc surroundings đóng vai trò quyết định.
=> Đáp án: NO

questions.prompt: Most people have the potential to be creative.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
most people
potential to be creative
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 2:
“almost every individual can be creative in the right circumstances.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
almost every individual = hầu hết mọi người
can be creative = có tiềm năng sáng tạo
=> Đáp án: YES

questions.prompt: Teams work best when their members are of equally matched intelligence.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
teams work best
equally matched intelligence
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 6:
“three people will be better than one... even if that one person is the smartest person in the field.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Bài chỉ nói teamwork hiệu quả hơn cá nhân xuất sắc.
Không nói các thành viên phải có trí tuệ ngang nhau.
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

questions.prompt: It is easier for smaller companies to be innovative.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
smaller companies
more innovative
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Toàn bài
Bước 3: Phân tích
Không có thông tin so sánh công ty nhỏ với công ty lớn về khả năng đổi mới.
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

questions.prompt: A manager's approval of an idea is more persuasive than that of a colleague.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
manager’s approval
more persuasive
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn 7:
“peer power... is much more powerful than any boss’s speech.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
peer power mạnh hơn lời nói của sếp.
=> câu đề bài trái với thông tin bài đọc.
=> Đáp án: NO
